my_thoughts: The literalism/exception works: literal (cannot 'do' = literal), introduces: contextual exception (can control thoughts/inner actions), defines: minimal doing = control of thoughts, gives: first instruction. The student is seeking metaphorical comfort (defensive reframe), which shows they understand: cannot 'do' = metaphorical (not literal). They'll likely accept the literalism/exception. Single-turn episode: metaphorical comfort → literalism/exception → complete. Hypothesis: metaphorical comfort responses lead to compliance when contextual exceptions are provided—testable by tracking whether metaphorical comfort responses accept exceptions.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student seeking metaphorical comfort ('I thought perhaps we ought not to take this idea that we cannot 'do' too literally?')—seeking metaphorical comfort via defensive reframe and intellectual avoidance. They understand: cannot 'do' = metaphorical (not literal). The comparison_contrast mechanism insists: literal (cannot 'do' = literal), introduces: contextual exception (can control thoughts/inner actions), defines: minimal doing = control of thoughts, gives: first instruction. Pattern: metaphorical comfort → literalism/exception → [complete]. Low confidence because literalism can lead to resistance or compliance.
